original_abstract:
In most natural and engineered systems, entities interact through intricate patterns that span multiple types of relationships, evolve over time, and involve various systemic layers. To understand these complex systems, it is essential to move beyond traditional network models and incorporate these "multilayer" features into our analyses. This necessitates generalizing classical network theory by developing and validating a comprehensive framework and a suite of associated tools. While efforts to study such structures date back several decades across diverse disciplines, the study of multilayer networks has recently emerged as a cornerstone of modern network science.

In this paper, we trace the history of multilayer networks and review the rapidly expanding body of literature in the field. To reconcile the disparate terminology found in recent studies, we propose a unified general framework and provide a detailed "dictionary" that relates and translates between various concepts. We offer a thorough comparison of related notions, including multiplex networks, interdependent networks, and networks of networks. Furthermore, we survey existing datasets that can be modeled as multilayer structures and review the development of diagnostic tools generalized from single-layer networks. Finally, we discuss the burgeoning research on multilayer models, community structure, tensor decompositions, and dynamical processes, concluding with a summary of the field’s current state and an outlook on future directions.
humanized_abstract:
Entities in most natural and engineered systems interact in a multiplicity of ways, forming complex patterns of associations that may change over time and occur across multiple systemic levels. Moving beyond the traditional network models to consider the multilayer nature of complex systems is a crucial step towards their comprehension. Thus, it is necessary to extend classical network science into new avenues of theory development and tool implementation. The fact that thinking about multiscale relations in complex systems has started several decades ago in various fields, and recently multilayer networks have started to play a central role in contemporary network science, strongly supports the urgent need for the advancements described in this chapter.

This paper provides a review of the history of multilayer networks and an update on the literature related to this important subject. We introduce a unified general framework that accounts for the majority of the definitions recently proposed, along with a dictionary to translate among the various terms and concepts. After discussing multiplex, interdependent and network of networks models and describing existing multilayer network datasets, we discuss existing graphical and metrics-based methods derived from single layer networks for analyzing multilayer networks, and then discuss in some details the multilayer models, community detection, tensor decomposition and dynamics existing in the new, rapidly growing, multilayer literature.
